english: pa (pa) Glr. abounding in tree-fruit; mód-po (mod po) adj. plentiful, abounding, kúl-lu-ru śiṅ mód-po (kul lu ru shing mod po) in Kullu wood is plentiful, or śiṅ mód-poi yul (shing mod poi yul) (Kullu is) a country abounding in wood, opp. to dkón-po (dkon po), hence 'cheap' may occasionally stand for it.

1. n. p., general name for the different nations living between Tibet and the Indian plain Mil: món-yul-gyi báṅ-dhe (mon yul gyi bang dhe) a monk from Nepal; Glr.: dpal-gro món-la (dpal gro mon la) Paldo in Bhotan; mon-ta-waṅ (mon ta wang) is stated to be a commercial place in Assam, from whence much rice is brought to Tibet; the people of Lahoul are looked upon by the real Tibetans as Mon, though for the most part they speak the Tibetan language, and they in their turn consider the Hindoos in Kullu as Mon; that this appellation is often extended to the Hindoos in general, appears from such names as, món-sraṅ (mon srang), món-sran (mon sran) Indian pea, Phaseolus radiatus, माष; món-ča-ra (mon ca ra) the ever-green oak and its fruit, of the southern Himalaya ridges Wdn.; in Ld. the musicians (Ld.-Glr. Schl. 25, b), carpenters, and wood-cutters coming from the south, are likewise denominated Mon. - The form mon-pa (mon pa) Cs. is not known to me; mon-mo (mon mo) fem. Pth. - 2. sometimes for mun (mun).
(or perh. rźa (rzha)) W., popularity, respect, reputation, món-źa tob (mon zha thob) he makes himself generally beloved, is highly respected; món-źa-čan (mon zha can) beloved, popular.

the usual resp. term, esp. in W., for to say, to speak, as bsgó-ba (bsgo ba) and bká-rtsal-ba (bka' rtsal ba) are used in earlier, and gsuṅ-ba (gsung ba) in later literature and in C., hence it is often to be rendered by 'to order'; *sá-heb-la sa-lám mol źu* (sa heb la sa lam mol zhu) have the goodness to present (say) my compliments to that gentleman; *mól-lče táṅ-če* (mol lce tang ce) to flatter, to caress; *mól-la táṅ-wa* (mol la tang wa) C. to make known(?).

vb. and sbst. to be pleased la (la) with, to wish, to have a mind, gró-bar mós-so (gro bar mos so) Glr. I took a fancy to go there; ču-la sógs-par mós-na (chu la sogs par mos na) Thgy. if you wish for water or something of the kind; mós-pa daṅ འdód-pa (mos pa dang 'dod pa) S.O. desiring and coveting (are the origin of all the misery of sin); to take pleasure in, to rejoice at, mós-pai glu (mos pai glu) Glr. song of rejoicing; as sbst.: pleasure, satisfaction, esteem. - 2. to respect, to esteem, with la (la), to respect with devotion, to revere, to adore čós-la (chos la) frq.; kyod gáṅ-la mos to (khyod gang la mos to) whom do you direct your devotions? Mil.; mós-nas འbúl-ba yin (mos nas 'bul ba yin) I give it merely from devout veneration, i.e. I shall take nothing for it Pth.; frq. joined with gús-pa (gus pa): yid-mos-gús drág-pos (yid mos gus drag pos) with fervent veneration; dad-mós (dad mos) devotion; mos spyód-pa (mos spyod pa) as participle, a pious man, a devotee Tar. 109, 7.

trouble, misery, affliction, mya-ṅán-gyis gdúṅ-ste (mya ngan gyis gdung ste) Dzl.; mya-ṅán či yaṅ med (mya ngan ci yang med) Dzl. I have no trouble, no uneasiness, whatever; mya-ṅán bsal (mya ngan bsal) Tar. the time of mourning is at an end; mya-ṅán byéd-pa (mya ngan byed pa) to lament, to wail; mya-ṅán-méd (mya ngan med), अशोक n. of a famous king of ancient India Glr., Tar. ch. VI; mya-ṅán-las འdás-pa (mya ngan las 'das pa), abbr. myaṅ-འdas (myang 'das) (and so also pronounced, as for instance in a verse of Mil., where it occurs as a trochee) 'having been delivered from pain', the usual, illiteral, Tibetan version of निर्वाण, the absolute cessation of all motion and excitement both of body and mind, which is necessarily connected with personal existence; absolute rest, which by orientals is thought to be the highest degree of happiness, imagined by some as a perfect annihilation of existence, by others, more or less, only as a cessation of all that is unpleasant in human existence, - well set forth by Köpp. I. 304 sqq.

Sch. 'to chew'; acc. to medical writings, the chemical decomposition of the chyme in the stomach; to cause putrefaction; pf. myags (myags); myágs-par byéd-pa (myags par byed pa) = myag-pa (myag pa) S.g.; rul-čiṅ myágs-pa (rul cing myags pa) Dzl. decomposed, putrefied; ro-myágs (ro myags) the watery product of putrefaction, 'tabes' Thgy.
